variables.content: Because direct thermodynamic-limit extrapolation of the discrete-angle DMC-determined Fermi liquid parameters proved unreliable, a set of “best available” numerical recommendations for the Fermi liquid parameters was assembled by combining independently published QMC results for related quantities: quasiparticle effective masses $m^{*}$ obtained from DMC energy-band calculations and smooth QMC-based parameterizations of the ground-state correlation energy.  Using the literature effective masses together with compressibility and spin-susceptibility values derived from the total-energy parameterizations and the Landau relations yields recommended values such as, for the paramagnetic 2D HEG: at $r_{s}=1$, $F_{0}^{s}=-0.495(2)$, $F_{0}^{a}=-0.346(2)$, $F_{1}^{s}=-0.053(3)$; at $r_{s}=5$ and $10$ analogous recommended values are provided; and for the ferromagnetic 2D HEG recommended values include at $r_{s}=1$, $F_{0}=-0.428(2)$ and $\bar{F}_1=-0.159(3)$.  These assembled values combine ground-state energy parameterizations and published effective masses to provide pragmatic numerical recommendations when direct thermodynamic-limit determinations of $F_l$ from discrete-angle DMC data are unreliable.

The Fermi liquid parameters divided by the quasiparticle effective mass, $\;F_{l}^{s,a}/m^{*}$ (symmetric and antisymmetric channels for the paramagnetic HEG) and $F_{l}/m^{*}$ (ferromagnetic HEG), were obtained from the discrete-angle DMC interaction functions by first computing the Fourier components
      $$
      a_{l}^{\sigma\sigma'}=\frac{2}{\pi}\int_{0}^{\pi}N f_{\sigma\sigma'}(\theta)\cos(l\theta)\,d\theta,
      $$
      and then using the relations for an $N$-electron paramagnetic finite cell of density parameter $r_s$
      $$
      \frac{F_{l}^{s,a}}{m^{*}}=\frac{r_s^2}{4}\big[a_{l}^{\uparrow\uparrow}\pm a_{l}^{\uparrow\downarrow}\big],
      $$
      and for a ferromagnetic finite cell
      $$
      \frac{F_l}{m^{*}}=\frac{r_s^2}{4}a_{l}^{\uparrow\uparrow},
      $$
      where $m^{*}$ denotes the quasiparticle effective mass.  Numerical angular integration of the available nonuniform discrete angles $\{\theta_i\}$ was performed using a generalized composite Simpson's rule (piecewise quadratic interpolation adapted to a nonuniform grid), and the result was compared with a generalized composite trapezoidal rule to place an upper bound on the quadrature error.  Finite-size values of $F_l/m^{*}$ obtained at multiple closed-shell electron counts were extrapolated to the thermodynamic limit $N\to\infty$ by fitting the finite-$N$ data to the model $F_l(N)=c+aN^{\gamma}$ with the exponent fixed at $\gamma=-1/4$ (i.e., leading finite-size correction $\propto N^{-1/4}$) motivated by theoretical analyses of long-range-correlation contributions; ordinary (unweighted) least-squares fits were used because oscillatory shell-filling deviations of the finite-size data are larger than the nominal Monte Carlo error bars, and the quoted uncertainties on the extrapolated parameters reflect the scatter of the finite-$N$ data under these fits.

The quasiparticle effective masses m^{*} reported in the literature from DMC fits to the single-particle energy band and the ground-state total-energy parametrizations reported in the literature as continuous functions E_{\mathrm{corr}}(r_s,\zeta) of density parameter r_s and spin polarization \zeta are mutually consistent within their stated uncertainties and can be combined algebraically via standard Fermi-liquid thermodynamic relations (for example F_1^{s} = m^{*}-1 for the paramagnetic HEG and evaluation of F_0^{s,a} from derivatives of E(r_s,\zeta)) to produce numerical estimates of the dimensionless Fermi liquid parameters F_l for the 2D HEG.
      Refs. 11,23,24

The diffusion Monte Carlo data for Fourier-projected Fermi liquid quantities display large, nonmonotonic fluctuations as functions of finite-system particle number N that exceed the per-run statistical error bars: the statistical uncertainties from individual DMC runs are generally much smaller than the observed shell-filling oscillations and other finite-size "noise." A theoretical finite-size scaling proportional to N^{-1/4} (the exponent -1/4 predicted for long-range-correlation-dominated finite-size errors) is consistent with all DMC data in a global fit (the best-fit common exponent found by simultaneous \chi^2 fitting of the DMC data is \gamma = -0.24(10)), but the fit quality is poor (\chi^2 per data point \gg 1) because the shell-filling oscillations and related finite-size noise are not captured by the statistical error bars; a naive ordinary least-squares fit with equal weighting of data points yields a very different effective exponent. Consequently, these finite-size effects constitute a serious obstacle to a quantitatively accurate, direct determination of Fermi liquid parameters from finite-cell QMC calculations: extrapolated Fermi liquid parameters obtained by simple power-law fits have large uncertainties and in many cases are inconsistent with related thermodynamic quantities obtained directly from parametrizations of the ground-state total energy per electron.

For a sequence of computed finite-size quasiparticle effective masses \\(m^{*}(N)\\) obtained by DMC for a 2D HEG at fixed density and spin polarization over the available range of system sizes \\(N\\), the proposition is: the dependence of \\(m^{*}(N)\\) on \\(N\\) can be modelled to good approximation by a single-term power-law finite-size correction,
      $$
      m^{*}(N)=m^{*}(\\infty)+b\\,N^{-\\gamma},
      $$
      with approximately constant parameters \\(m^{*}(\\infty)\\), \\(b\\), and exponent \\(\\gamma\\) across the sampled \\(N\\)-range, so that this parametric form provides a valid basis for extrapolation to the thermodynamic limit within the statistical precision of the DMC data.

The Landau interaction functions f_{\sigma\sigma'}(\theta) for a two-dimensional homogeneous electron gas (2D HEG) were evaluated at a discrete set of angles \theta using diffusion Monte Carlo (DMC) total-energy differences according to the estimator
      $$
      f_{\sigma\sigma'}(\theta_i)=-\big[E_{\sigma\sigma'}(\mathbf{k}_i,\mathbf{k}_i')+E_0-E_{+}(\mathbf{k}_i)-E_{-}(\mathbf{k}_i')\big],
      $$
      where $E_0$ denotes the ground-state total energy per simulation cell, $E_{\sigma\sigma'}(\mathbf{k}_i,\mathbf{k}_i')$ denotes the total energy of the finite-cell excited state obtained by promoting an electron from wavevector $\mathbf{k}_i'$ just inside the Fermi surface to wavevector $\mathbf{k}_i$ just outside it (the angle between $\mathbf{k}_i$ and $\mathbf{k}_i'$ is $\theta_i$), and $E_{+}(\mathbf{k}_i)$ and $E_{-}(\mathbf{k}_i')$ denote the total energies of the finite cell with one electron added at $\mathbf{k}_i$ and one electron removed from $\mathbf{k}_i'$, respectively; here DMC denotes the projector Monte Carlo simulation in imaginary time performed with the fixed-node approximation.  These DMC-calculated discrete-angle interaction functions were obtained for unpolarized (paramagnetic, spin polarization $\zeta=0$) and fully spin-polarized (ferromagnetic, $\zeta=1$) 2D HEGs at density parameters $r_{s}=1$, $5$, and $10$ using square periodic simulation cells; the results are presented as plots of $N\,f_{\sigma\sigma'}(\theta)$ versus $\theta$, where $N$ is the number of electrons in the simulation cell.  The DMC data sets include multiple finite system sizes chosen as closed-shell (“magic”) electron counts and report values separately for the parallel-spin ($\sigma=\sigma'$) and antiparallel-spin ($\sigma\neq\sigma'$) channels.

For the same HEG systems and trial wave functions defined above, applying the three-parameter extrapolation model
      $E_N = E_\infty + b_1\,\Delta T_{\mathrm{HF}}(N) + b_2/N$
      to DMC energies but fixing the finite-size coefficients $b_1$ and $b_2$ to the values obtained from VMC fits produces extrapolated DMC thermodynamic-limit energies that are spuriously low compared to extrapolations obtained by fitting $b_1$ and $b_2$ directly to the DMC data: using VMC $b_1,b_2$ yields DMC extrapolations DMC-SJ* $E_\infty^{\mathrm{DMC-SJ*}} = 0.00266(1)$ Ha/e and DMC-SJB* $E_\infty^{\mathrm{DMC-SJB*}} = 0.001380(8)$ Ha/e, whereas fitting $b_1,b_2$ to the DMC data gives DMC-SJ $E_\infty^{\mathrm{DMC-SJ}} = 0.00301(1)$ Ha/e and DMC-SJB $E_\infty^{\mathrm{DMC-SJB}} = 0.001621(7)$ Ha/e; therefore the hypothesis that VMC and DMC share identical finite-size dependences ($b_1,b_2$) is invalid for these systems and reusing VMC parameters biases DMC $E_\infty$ downward beyond the quoted uncertainties.

The occupied single-particle energy band $\mathcal{E}(k)$ of the interacting two-dimensional homogeneous electron gas (2D HEG) was computed using the diffusion quantum Monte Carlo (DMC) method by taking total-energy differences on adding or removing a single electron in a plane-wave orbital $\exp(i\mathbf{k}\!\cdot\!\mathbf{r})$; the resulting discrete DMC band values for the occupied states were described by a least-squares fit to the quartic form $\mathcal{E}(k)=\alpha_{0}+\alpha_{2}k^{2}+\alpha_{4}k^{4}$, where $\alpha_{0}$, $\alpha_{2}$, and $\alpha_{4}$ are fitted coefficients.  These DMC calculations were performed for both the paramagnetic (unpolarized) and the ferromagnetic (fully spin-polarized) 2D HEGs at density-parameter values $r_{s}=1$, $5$, and $10$ (atomic units, where lengths are given in Bohr radii).  The fitted quartic shows clear nonquadratic behavior: for the paramagnetic fluid $\alpha_{4}>0$ at $r_{s}=5$ and $r_{s}=10$, for the ferromagnetic fluid $\alpha_{4}<0$ at all three densities studied, and for the paramagnetic fluid at $r_{s}=1$ the fitted DMC band is very close to the free-electron dispersion $\mathcal{E}(k)=k^{2}/2$ (all energies in Hartree atomic units).

Reoptimization of the explicit correlation parameters in finite-cell excited-state trial wave functions produces a measurable lowering of the DMC total energy of that finite cell and thereby increases the finite-size bias of excitation energies computed as total-energy differences. Concretely, when the Jastrow-factor and backflow-function parameters were reoptimized after removing an electron from wavevector \\(\\mathbf{k}=0\\) in a paramagnetic 26-electron 2D HEG at density parameter \\(r_{s}=1\\), the DMC total energy was lowered by 0.000854(4) atomic units (a.u.), which reduced the finite-cell bandwidth and therefore increased the finite-size error in the derived excitation energies. Consequently, to avoid introducing this additional finite-cell bias into excitation-energy estimates, the Jastrow and backflow parameters were optimized once in the closed-shell ground state for each density and system size and then held fixed for all corresponding excited-state DMC calculations.
